content:
First lines of real JavaScript — Variables and Data Types.

4 min read
Just now

--

“Every JavaScript program starts with data. Before writing logic, we first need a way to store and work with information.”
We Are Actually Writing Code Now
Part 1 — I chose JavaScript.
Part 2 — I understood how it works.
Part 3 — I finally opened my code editor and wrote something.
And honestly? It felt like the first time I wrote a test case. A little unsure. A little excited. But once it clicked — there was no going back.
This week I learned two of the most fundamental building blocks of JavaScript: Variables and Data Types.
Everything in JavaScript starts here.

What Is a Variable?
Think of a variable like a storage box or container that stores information .
You give the box a name. You put something inside it. And whenever you need that thing — you just call the box by its name.
In testing terms — think of it like a test data holder.
Instead of hardcoding the same email address in 10 different places, you store it once in a variable and use it everywhere.
let email = "greeshma@example.com";Now wherever you need that email — you just use email.
Simple. Clean. Easy to update.
Three Ways to Create a Variable
This is where JavaScript gets interesting and slightly confusing at first.
JavaScript has three keywords for creating variables: var, let, and const.

• var is function-scoped, can be redeclared and reassigned.

• let is block-scoped, cannot be redeclared, but can be reassigned.

• const is block-scoped, cannot be redeclared or reassigned.
The simple rule I follow:

• Use const by default — when you know the value won't change

• Use let — when the value needs to change later

• Avoid var — it's outdated and causes unexpected bugs
Real QA Example — How I Think About This
const baseURL = "https://myapp.com"; // Never changes
const username = "admin"; // Never changeslet loginAttempts = 0; // Will change during test
let testStatus = "not started"; // Will change during testAs a QA Engineer this immediately made sense.
Test environment URLs — const. They don't change. Test counters, statuses, dynamic values — let. They change as the test runs.
What Are Data Types?
Now that I knew how to create a variable — the next question was:
What kind of data can I put inside it?
JavaScript has several data types. This week I focused on the three most common ones. They are
Strings — Text Data
A string is simply text — wrapped in quotes.
const pageName = "Login Page";
const errorMessage = "Invalid credentials";
const apiEndpoint = "/api/v1/login";You can use single quotes, double quotes, or backticks — all valid in JavaScript.
Style Example When to Use Double quotes "Login Page" General use Single quotes 'Login Page' General use Backticks `Login Page` When combining text + variables
Backticks are especially useful in testing:
const userId = 101;
console.log(`Testing user ID: ${userId}`);
// Output: Testing user ID: 101Numbers — Numeric Data
JavaScript keeps it simple — there is only one type for all numbers.
const statusCode = 200;
const responseTime = 1.5;
const failedTests = 0;No separate integer or float types like other languages. One type handle everything.
QA use case:
const expectedStatusCode = 200;
const actualStatusCode = 201;console.log(expectedStatusCode === actualStatusCode);
// Output: false — test would fail ❌Booleans — True or False
This one felt the most natural as a QA Engineer.
A boolean holds only one of two values — true or false.
const isLoggedIn = true;
const isPageLoaded = false;
const hasError = true;Think of it as a pass/fail flag.
Scenario Boolean Value Test passed true Test failed false User is logged in true Error found on page true API returned 200 true
In automation — booleans are everywhere. Every assertion you write is ultimately checking whether something is true or false.
Putting It All Together
Here’s a small snapshot of how variables and data types work together — written the way a QA Engineer would think:
// Test setup
const baseURL = "https://myapp.com";
const testUsername = "admin";
const testPassword = "Admin@123";// Test state
let isLoggedIn = false;
let statusCode = 0;
let responseMessage = "";// After login action
statusCode = 200;
responseMessage = "Login successful";
isLoggedIn = true;console.log(isLoggedIn); // true
console.log(statusCode); // 200
console.log(responseMessage); // Login successfulNothing complex. Just clean, readable code — that any QA Engineer can follow.
What I Learned This Week
✅ Variables are storage boxes that hold data
✅ const — for values that never change
✅ let — for values that change during execution
✅ var — outdated, avoid using it
✅ Strings store text data
✅ Numbers handle all numeric values — no separate types
✅ Booleans store only true or false — the foundation of every assertion
Every test you write stores data somewhere. Variables are how JavaScript holds that data. Data types are what that data looks like. Master these — and everything else builds on top.
If this series is helping you — share it with a QA Engineer who is on the same journey.

👋 I’m Greeshma — a QA Engineer with 7+ years of experience, learning JavaScript in public one week at a time. Follow me so you don’t miss Part 4.
